refined_fr.refined_fr: Enable measurement and reporting of material throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the system's capability to measure/report throughput metrics, and (2) the achievement of a customer-defined target. The latter is a success criterion dependent on operational context, training, and external factors beyond system design. The refined FR isolates the system's functional obligation—providing accurate, timely throughput data—which is solution-neutral and testable independent of target achievement. The phrase 'support achieving' is vague and mixes design intent with acceptance criteria; 'enable measurement and reporting' is precise and in the problem domain.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct instrumented test runs under controlled conditions (representative material type, equipment configuration, operator skill level) and verify that reported throughput values match independently measured reference values within ±5% accuracy. Confirm data is logged and retrievable for post-operation analysis.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-001.1: Acquire real-time material throughput data from operational sensors.
FR-NGHE-001.2: Calculate and aggregate throughput metrics (m³/h or ton/h) from acquired data.
FR-NGHE-001.3: Display current and historical throughput metrics to authorized users in real time or near-real time.
FR-NGHE-001.4: Log and store throughput data with sufficient fidelity and retention for post-operation analysis and reporting.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire instantaneous flow rate via continuous sensing of material stream properties (mass or volume per unit time) and integrate over evaluation period to compute cumulative throughput
DP2: Periodically sample material quantity at fixed time intervals, compute differential changes, and extrapolate to period-level throughput estimate
DP3: Instrument material input and output boundaries with independent accumulation counters (mass or volume), compute net throughput as difference, and normalize by elapsed time
DP4: Establish reference material batches of known quantity, measure batch processing rate under representative conditions, and scale to full-period throughput projection
DP5: Decompose throughput into constituent process stages (e.g., feed rate, processing rate, discharge rate), measure each independently, and aggregate via process model to infer system throughput
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP3: Instrument material input and output boundaries with independent accumulation counters (mass or volume), compute net throughput as difference, and normalize by elapsed time

potential_sources: Boundary definition coupling: Input and output measurement points must be spatially and temporally well-defined to avoid double-counting or loss attribution ambiguity
Accumulator synchronization: If input and output counters operate asynchronously, clock drift or timing misalignment could introduce systematic bias
Material hold-up: Transient material in-process (e.g., in hoppers, conveyors) creates interpretation ambiguity—net throughput reflects steady-state flow but not instantaneous rate variability
Sensor independence: Input and output sensors must be decoupled in failure modes to prevent correlated measurement errors masking process faults
coupling_check.notes: DP3 exhibits low functional coupling because input and output measurement are logically independent; the subtraction operation is deterministic and decoupled from process internals. However, architectural coupling risk exists if sensors share power, calibration, or data infrastructure. Recommend independent sensor chains and validation protocols.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Material accumulation can be measured with sufficient precision and accuracy at system boundaries to meet acceptance criteria tolerance (implicit in `≥ productivity_target`)
Risk: If acceptance criteria tolerance is tight (e.g., ±2%), sensor accuracy and drift over evaluation period may be insufficient; recommend pre-evaluation calibration and drift characterization
Assumption: Evaluation period is long enough that transient start-up and shut-down effects are negligible relative to steady-state throughput
Risk: Short evaluation windows or frequent start-stop cycles may bias results; recommend minimum evaluation duration specification
Assumption: Material properties (density, moisture, particle size) remain stable during evaluation; significant variation could invalidate mass-to-volume conversions
Risk: Environmental conditions (temperature, humidity) or feedstock variability could introduce systematic error; recommend environmental monitoring and feedstock specification
Assumption: Worksite safety plan permits installation of measurement instrumentation at input/output boundaries without operational disruption
Risk: Access constraints, explosive atmospheres, or hygiene requirements may limit sensor placement options; recommend early safety review
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to specify: (1) acceptable measurement uncertainty (e.g., ±5% of productivity_target), (2) evaluation period duration and conditions (steady-state vs. transient), (3) material property ranges (mass density, moisture content, particle size distribution), (4) boundary definitions (e.g., 'material entering hopper' vs. 'material exiting discharge chute'), and (5) reporting frequency and format. This will enable DP selection to be validated against quantitative acceptance criteria and reduce interpretation ambiguity during implementation.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized material flow monitoring and throughput computation subsystem that instruments input/output boundaries, maintains independent accumulation counters, and derives net throughput metrics

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmit computed net throughput, accumulation counter states, and material flow metrics to monitoring/logging systems

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receive configuration parameters for counter initialization, boundary definitions, and throughput normalization intervals

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Signal anomalous throughput deviations or counter inconsistencies that may indicate material loss or system malfunction

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: material flow instrumentation and throughput computation are core NGHE Solution responsibilities. Counter logic, accumulation, and normalization are self-contained. Minor diffusion: SafetyAlert and Telemetry interfaces require external coupling, but this is expected and minimal.

Poor fit: Vehicle is a physical platform, not a logical processing boundary. Material flow monitoring is orthogonal to vehicle control. Would require Vehicle to own domain logic that belongs in NGHE Solution. High diffusion: counter state, throughput computation, and normalization would leak across multiple vehicle subsystems.

Poor fit: Teleoperation is a command/control interface, not a material flow monitoring subsystem. Placing accumulation counters and throughput logic here violates separation of concerns. High diffusion: would require Teleoperation to manage state unrelated to operator commands.

Weak fit: Safety Services consumes throughput anomalies but should not own the instrumentation and computation logic. Placing counter accumulation here conflates safety response with material flow measurement. Moderate diffusion: Safety Services would need to maintain state and perform calculations outside its primary responsibility domain.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The DP (independent accumulation counters, net throughput computation, time normalization) is a core material flow measurement and analysis function that logically belongs in the NGHE Solution subsystem. This subsystem is responsible for monitoring and optimizing material handling processes; instrumentation and throughput metrics are essential to that mission. Containment is strong because all counter state, accumulation logic, and normalization algorithms remain within a single cohesive boundary. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert) are minimal and well-defined, representing expected information flow rather than logic diffusion.
containment_risks: If counter sensor inputs are distributed across Vehicle subsystems, integration points may proliferate and create hidden coupling.
If SafetyAlert thresholds or anomaly detection logic is not clearly specified, Safety Services may attempt to own parts of the throughput computation, fragmenting the DP.
If Telemetry interface does not provide sufficient bandwidth or latency guarantees, real-time throughput updates may be delayed, weakening the DP's effectiveness.
If Tasking interface does not support dynamic reconfiguration of counter boundaries or normalization intervals, the DP may become rigid and difficult to adapt.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR (enable measurement and reporting of material throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time) is directly measurable against acceptance criteria: reported productivity ≥ productivity_target over evaluation period. The IM boundary (NGHE Solution) owns the complete measurement pipeline (input/output instrumentation, accumulation counters, net throughput computation, time normalization). Verification can be conducted by comparing NGHE Solution telemetry output against ground-truth material flow logs and target thresholds.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The FR is unambiguously allocated to NGHE Solution with well-defined external interfaces (Telemetry out, Tasking in, SafetyAlert out). No competing ownership claims exist among Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services. The scope statement explicitly binds instrumentation, counter maintenance, and metric derivation to NGHE Solution.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The DP (independent accumulation counters at input/output boundaries, net throughput as difference, time normalization) is a sound and complete solution to the FR. It directly addresses the functional requirement by instrumenting material flow boundaries and computing throughput in standard units per unit time. The logic is decomposable (counter initialization, accumulation, differencing, normalization) and testable. No logical gaps or contradictions detected.

diffusion_risks: Counter sensor inputs: If physical sensor wiring or signal conditioning is distributed across Vehicle subsystems rather than routed through NGHE Solution, integration points will proliferate and create hidden coupling. Mitigation: Establish a single sensor harness or data acquisition interface owned by NGHE Solution.

SafetyAlert threshold logic: If anomaly detection thresholds or deviation criteria are defined in Safety Services rather than computed within NGHE Solution and passed as alerts, throughput computation logic will fragment. Mitigation: NGHE Solution must own all threshold definitions and anomaly detection; Safety Services consumes only the alert signal.

Telemetry bandwidth/latency: If the Telemetry interface cannot guarantee real-time or near-real-time delivery of throughput metrics, external systems may attempt to replicate counter logic locally, creating diffusion. Mitigation: Specify Telemetry SLA (e.g., <100ms latency, ≥10 Hz update rate) in interface contract.

Tasking reconfiguration: If Tasking interface does not support dynamic updates to counter boundaries, normalization intervals, or accumulation reset commands, operational flexibility will be constrained and workarounds may bypass NGHE Solution. Mitigation: Define Tasking API to include boundary redefinition, interval adjustment, and state reset operations.

Counter state persistence: If counter state (accumulated mass/volume, timestamps) is not clearly owned and persisted within NGHE Solution, external modules may attempt to cache or replicate state, creating inconsistency. Mitigation: Establish NGHE Solution as single source of truth for counter state; provide read-only query interface to external consumers.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). The DP is tightly bound to NGHE Solution: all accumulation logic, differencing, and normalization remain within a single cohesive boundary. Counter state is owned by NGHE Solution; external interfaces are information flows (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert), not logic diffusion. The three alternative modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) all show poor fit (0.28–0.45 containment) and high diffusion (0.55–0.72), confirming that NGHE Solution is the correct allocation. Minor diffusion risk exists only at interface boundaries (sensor inputs, alert thresholds, telemetry delivery), which is expected and manageable with clear interface contracts.

recommended_revisions: Specify Telemetry interface SLA: Define latency, update frequency, and data format (e.g., JSON with timestamp, counter states, net throughput, units) to ensure real-time visibility and prevent external replication of counter logic.

Define Tasking API for dynamic reconfiguration: Include operations for boundary redefinition, normalization interval adjustment, counter reset, and sensor enable/disable to support operational flexibility without bypassing NGHE Solution.

Establish sensor input ownership: Clarify whether physical sensors are wired directly to NGHE Solution or routed through Vehicle subsystems. If routed through Vehicle, define a single data acquisition interface (e.g., CAN bus, Ethernet) owned by NGHE Solution to prevent diffusion.

Specify SafetyAlert anomaly detection: Define thresholds, deviation criteria, and alert conditions within NGHE Solution; Safety Services shall consume only the alert signal, not replicate detection logic.

Document counter state persistence: Specify how counter state (accumulated mass/volume, timestamps, boundary definitions) is stored, recovered after power loss, and queried by external systems. Establish NGHE Solution as single source of truth.

Add acceptance criterion for throughput accuracy: Complement the productivity target criterion with a measurement accuracy requirement (e.g., ±5% error vs. ground-truth material flow) to verify DP effectiveness and sensor calibration.
Define counter initialization and calibration procedure: Specify how counters are zeroed, how sensor offsets are applied, and how calibration is validated before operations begin.

decision.justification: Gate results confirm FR, DP, and IM are logically sound and well-allocated (all three gates pass with high confidence). However, the recommended_revisions and diffusion_risks expose critical interface and state-management concerns that must be decomposed into sub-FRs before detailed design. The high safety criticality factor amplifies the need for explicit functional requirements governing sensor input ownership, anomaly detection logic, telemetry delivery guarantees, and counter state persistence. These are not minor implementation details—they are functional requirements that prevent logic diffusion and ensure safety-critical throughput measurement is not compromised by external workarounds or inconsistent state. Decomposition is necessary to allocate these interface and persistence concerns to specific sub-modules and establish verifiable acceptance criteria.
next_level_sub_frs: FR 1.1: Acquire and condition material flow sensor signals at input and output boundaries with single-point ownership and no external replication.
FR 1.2: Accumulate material mass or volume at input and output boundaries with state persistence, recovery after power loss, and read-only external query interface.
FR 1.3: Compute net throughput as the difference between output and input accumulation counters, normalized to standard units per unit time.
FR 1.4: Detect anomalies in throughput (e.g., deviation from expected range, sensor failure, counter overflow) and generate safety alerts without delegating detection logic to external modules.
FR 1.5: Deliver throughput metrics and counter state to external consumers (Teleoperation, Vehicle, Safety Services) with guaranteed latency, update frequency, and data consistency.

FR 1.6: Accept dynamic reconfiguration commands (counter boundary redefinition, normalization interval adjustment, counter reset, sensor enable/disable) from Tasking interface without loss of state or measurement continuity.
FR 1.7: Validate counter initialization, sensor calibration, and offset application before operations begin, with audit trail for safety compliance.

revise.why: The current DP statement ('independent accumulation counters at input/output boundaries, net throughput as difference, time normalization') is logically correct but under-specifies the critical design decisions required to prevent diffusion and ensure safety. Revision should explicitly address: (1) sensor signal acquisition and conditioning ownership (single harness vs. distributed routing), (2) counter state persistence mechanism (in-memory, persistent storage, recovery strategy), (3) anomaly detection logic location and threshold ownership, (4) telemetry interface SLA (latency, update rate, format), (5) tasking API for dynamic reconfiguration, and (6) calibration and initialization procedure. These design parameters are not implementation details—they are functional design choices that directly impact safety and architectural integrity. Revising the DP to include these choices will make the decomposition into sub-FRs more precise and verifiable.

assumptions: NGHE Solution has exclusive ownership of counter state and anomaly detection logic; no external module replicates or caches this state.
Sensor inputs are routed through a single data acquisition interface owned by NGHE Solution (e.g., CAN bus, Ethernet, or dedicated harness), not distributed across Vehicle subsystems.
Telemetry interface can guarantee real-time or near-real-time delivery (<100 ms latency, ≥10 Hz update rate) without external buffering or replication.
Tasking interface supports dynamic reconfiguration of counter boundaries, normalization intervals, and reset commands without requiring NGHE Solution restart.
Counter state is persisted in non-volatile storage within NGHE Solution and recovered automatically after power loss.
Safety Services consumes only SafetyAlert signals generated by NGHE Solution; it does not replicate anomaly detection logic.
Measurement accuracy requirement (±5% error vs. ground-truth material flow) is achievable with selected sensor technology and calibration procedure.
open_risks: Sensor signal integrity: If physical sensors are wired through Vehicle subsystems without clear ownership, signal conditioning and calibration may be inconsistent or duplicated. Mitigation: Establish single sensor harness owned by NGHE Solution; define signal conditioning and calibration procedure in DP revision.

Counter state consistency: If counter state is not persisted or recovered correctly after power loss, throughput metrics may become unreliable. Mitigation: Specify persistence mechanism (e.g., EEPROM, database) and recovery procedure in FR 1.2 decomposition.

Anomaly detection diffusion: If Safety Services or external modules attempt to replicate anomaly detection logic, thresholds may diverge and safety alerts may be inconsistent. Mitigation: Establish NGHE Solution as single source of truth for anomaly detection; define SafetyAlert interface contract in FR 1.4 decomposition.

Telemetry latency: If Telemetry interface cannot guarantee <100 ms latency, external systems may cache or replicate counter logic to avoid stale data. Mitigation: Specify Telemetry SLA in interface contract; monitor latency in operational testing.

Tasking API completeness: If Tasking interface does not support all required reconfiguration operations, operational flexibility will be constrained and workarounds may bypass NGHE Solution. Mitigation: Define comprehensive Tasking API in FR 1.6 decomposition; validate against operational use cases.

Calibration and initialization: If counter initialization and sensor calibration procedures are not clearly defined and audited, measurement accuracy may be compromised. Mitigation: Specify calibration procedure and audit trail in FR 1.7 decomposition.
evidence_needed: Sensor specification and signal conditioning design: Confirm that selected sensors meet accuracy requirement (±5% error) and that signal conditioning is owned by NGHE Solution.
Counter state persistence design: Specify storage mechanism, recovery procedure, and validation method for counter state after power loss.
Anomaly detection algorithm and thresholds: Define detection logic, threshold values, and alert conditions; validate against expected throughput ranges and failure modes.
Telemetry interface specification: Document latency, update frequency, data format, and error handling; measure actual latency in prototype or simulation.
Tasking API specification: Define all reconfiguration operations (boundary redefinition, interval adjustment, reset, sensor enable/disable); validate against operational scenarios.
Calibration and initialization procedure: Document step-by-step procedure, sensor offset application, and audit trail; validate in lab environment before field deployment.
Ground-truth material flow logs: Establish baseline for verification; compare NGHE Solution telemetry output against ground-truth logs to validate DP effectiveness and measurement accuracy.
